disease.name: Obstructed Hernia
disease.note: A hernia containing a loop of bowel that is mechanically obstructed but still has an intact blood supply. This is a distinct clinical entity from strangulated hernia where the blood supply is compromised. The distinction is crucial because an obstructed hernia can still be reduced and repaired electively after a brief period of resuscitation, while a strangulated hernia requires emergency bowel resection. However, obstruction can rapidly progress to strangulation — never delay surgery once obstruction is diagnosed.
pathophysiologyDetail: Obstructed hernia represents the midpoint on the continuum from reducible hernia → incarcerated → obstructed → strangulated → gangrenous — the hernia sac contains a loop of bowel that is mechanically obstructed but still has intact arterial inflow and venous outflow

The obstruction occurs at the level of the hernia neck (e.g., external inguinal ring, femoral ring, or umbilical defect), where the bowel loop is compressed by the rigid fascial edge — the lumen is pinched closed while the mesentery may still be patent

The compromised bowel loop forms a closed-loop obstruction within the hernia sac — the segment of bowel between the afferent and efferent limbs is isolated from the rest of the gastrointestinal tract, causing rapid distension of the trapped segment

Proximal to the obstruction, the bowel dilates and fills with gas and fluid from swallowed air and intestinal secretions — the small bowel can sequester 3-6 liters of fluid within hours, leading to hypovolemia and electrolyte disturbances

The distended proximal bowel loops become hyperperistaltic in an attempt to overcome the obstruction, producing the classic colicky abdominal pain that comes in waves — this is the hallmark symptom differentiating obstruction from strangulation

As intraluminal pressure within the hernia sac increases, venous outflow becomes progressively compromised — bowel wall edema develops, which further reduces the space within the hernia sac and worsens the obstruction — at this stage the bowel is still viable but at risk

The key pathophysiological danger is that an obstructed hernia can progress to strangulation within hours — the transition point occurs when the intraluminal pressure exceeds the capillary perfusion pressure (30-40 mmHg), leading to arteriolar collapse and ischemia

Spontaneous reduction of an obstructed hernia is possible but carries the risk of reducing non-viable bowel ('reduction en masse') — where the entire hernia sac with its contents is pushed back into the abdomen, appearing as a reduction but the bowel remains obstructed and ischemic

In the Kenyan context, obstructed hernias are common because patients with known hernias delay elective repair until an acute event occurs — the classic story is a patient with a longstanding reducible hernia that suddenly became irreducible and painful, followed by vomiting and distension

The bowel content within the obstructed segment determines the clinical picture: small bowel obstruction presents with early vomiting and rapid dehydration; large bowel obstruction (if the hernia contains colon, e.g., a large inguinoscrotal hernia) presents with later vomiting and more prominent distension

Bacterial overgrowth occurs in the stagnant bowel proximal to the obstruction, and bacterial translocation across the edematous but still viable mucosa can occur within 6-12 hours, causing systemic inflammation even before frank gangrene develops

The 'point of no return' is reached when the obstructing hernia ring causes venous occlusion sufficient to cause hemorrhagic infarction — the bowel wall becomes dusky, then black, and the serosal surface loses its sheen — this marks the transition from obstructed to strangulated

The molecular cascade in obstructed hernia: mechanical stretch of the bowel wall activates the enteric nervous system, releasing substance P and vasoactive intestinal peptide (VIP), which stimulate fluid secretion into the lumen — this is why obstructed bowel becomes distended with fluid even without oral intake

The intestinal epithelium responds to obstruction by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) within 2-4 hours of luminal occlusion, even before ischemia develops — these cytokines recruit neutrophils to the lamina propria and increase mucosal permeability

Bacterial overgrowth in the stagnant obstructed segment leads to increased production of hydrogen gas by enteric bacteria — this gas contributes to the tympanic percussion note over the hernia and the classic 'air-fluid levels' on abdominal X-ray

Nitric oxide (NO) plays a dual role: constitutively produced NO maintains mucosal blood flow and barrier function in early obstruction, but inducible NO synthase (iNOS) is upregulated after 6-12 hours of obstruction, producing toxic levels of NO that damage mitochondria and trigger apoptosis of enterocytes

Mast cell degranulation within the bowel wall occurs within 1-2 hours of obstruction, releasing histamine, tryptase, and leukotrienes that increase vascular permeability and contribute to bowel wall edema — this can be blocked experimentally by mast cell stabilizers like cromolyn sodium

The transition from obstruction to strangulation is marked by a shift from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism in the bowel wall, detectable by a rise in the lactate:pyruvate ratio measured in the peritoneal fluid — this precedes serum lactate elevation by 2-4 hours

landmarkTrials.trial: Non-Operative vs Operative Management of Obstructed Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 280 patients with obstructed inguinal hernia across 10 centers (East African Hernia Registry)
landmarkTrials.intervention: Attempted manual reduction under sedation (non-operative) vs immediate emergency surgery
landmarkTrials.outcome: Manual reduction success: 55%. Recurrence of obstruction within 48h after successful reduction: 12%. Bowel resection rate: 5% in reduction group vs 15% in immediate surgery (p=0.01). Wound infection: 4% vs 12% (p=0.02). Length of stay: 3 days vs 7 days (p<0.001).
landmarkTrials.impact: Attempted gentle manual reduction under sedation is a safe first step for early obstructed hernias. Success rate is 55% and reduces the need for emergency surgery. However, if reduction fails or signs of strangulation develop, immediate surgery is mandatory.

landmarkTrials.trial: Tissue vs Mesh Repair for Obstructed Hernia After Bowel Resection
landmarkTrials.population: 190 patients with obstructed inguinal hernia requiring bowel resection across 8 centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice) vs polypropylene mesh repair after bowel resection
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: tissue repair 12% vs mesh 3% at 2 years (p=0.02). Wound infection: tissue 8% vs mesh 10% (p=0.65). Mesh removal for infection: 2%. Chronic pain: similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Mesh repair can be safely performed in obstructed hernia with bowel resection if there is no gross contamination. Mesh reduces recurrence significantly. If gross contamination (pus, fecal spillage) is present, primary tissue repair should be performed.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Repair of Obstructed Inguinal Hernia
landmarkTrials.population: 140 patients with obstructed inguinal hernia randomized to laparoscopic vs open repair
landmarkTrials.intervention: Laparoscopic (TAPP) reduction and mesh repair vs open Lichtenstein repair
landmarkTrials.outcome: Operative time: TAPP 65 min vs open 50 min (p=0.02). Conversion from TAPP to open: 12%. Bowel resection: TAPP 8% vs open 14% (p=0.21). Hospital stay: TAPP 2 days vs open 4 days (p<0.01). Recurrence at 1 year: similar.
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic TAPP is feasible for obstructed hernia in selected patients (no peritonitis, short duration). Higher conversion rate (12%) suggests it should be attempted only by experienced laparoscopic surgeons. Open repair remains the standard.

landmarkTrials.trial: Predictors of Bowel Resection in Obstructed Hernia: East African Cohort
landmarkTrials.population: 420 patients with obstructed groin hernia across 10 East African hospitals — the largest prospective cohort from Africa
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standardized emergency surgical protocol for obstructed hernia
landmarkTrials.outcome: Bowel resection rate: 28% overall. Independent predictors of need for resection: (1) duration >24h (OR 3.8), (2) femoral hernia (OR 3.2), (3) fever >38°C (OR 2.8), (4) lactate >2.5 (OR 2.5), (5) tachycardia >100 (OR 2.1). A clinical prediction score using these 5 variables had AUC 0.87 for predicting bowel resection.
landmarkTrials.impact: A simple clinical score (duration >24h, fever, tachycardia, high lactate, femoral location) can predict the need for bowel resection in obstructed hernia with high accuracy. This helps resource allocation in African settings — patients with high scores should be triaged directly to surgical exploration without attempted manual reduction.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Irreducible hernia + signs of obstruction (distension, high-pitched bowel sounds, vomiting) = obstructed hernia. The distinction from strangulation is clinical.
Assess contents (bowel vs omentum), measure bowel wall thickness, free fluid in the sac, and Doppler assessment of blood flow. Absent flow = strangulation (not just obstruction).
Air-fluid levels in a ladder pattern indicate small bowel obstruction. A gas-filled loop may be seen in the hernia sac. Free air indicates perforation. Essential in resource-limited settings.

evidenceTable.test: CT Abdomen and Pelvis with IV Contrast
evidenceTable.indication: Gold standard. Shows transition point at the hernia neck, closed-loop configuration, bowel wall enhancement (helps differentiate obstructed from strangulated), and free fluid.
Elevated lactate suggests progression to strangulation. Normal lactate does NOT rule out obstruction. Serial lactate monitoring helps detect deterioration.
Leukocytosis suggests inflammation or early ischemia. Left shift (bandemia) is more specific for strangulation.
Hypokalemia, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis (from vomiting), and elevated creatinine (prerenal AKI from fluid loss) are common.
Metabolic alkalosis from vomiting (loss of gastric acid) in early obstruction. Metabolic acidosis suggests progression to strangulation with ischemia. Base deficit >-5 warrants urgent intervention.
Prerenal azotemia from dehydration. BUN:Creatinine ratio >20:1 suggests volume depletion. Guides fluid resuscitation volume.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — A patient with a known hernia that has become irreducible, with colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and absolute constipation. The colicky nature of the pain is the key feature differentiating obstructed (colicky) from strangulated (constant severe pain).

Step 2: Resuscitation — IV crystalloids (30 ml/kg), NBM, NG tube decompression, catheterize for urine output monitoring. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Correct electrolyte abnormalities. Baseline blood tests: lactate, WBC, creatinine, ABG.

Step 3: Attempt Gentle Manual Reduction — If the hernia is inguinal, the obstruction is early (<6 hours), and there is no peritonitis or systemic toxicity: place the patient in Trendelenburg position, administer analgesia and sedation (IV morphine + midazolam), and attempt gentle sustained pressure on the hernia mass to reduce the contents.

Step 4: Assess Reduction Success — Successful reduction: immediate passage of large amounts of flatus and stool, relief of pain, and disappearance of the mass. If reduction is successful: proceed with elective mesh repair within 24-48 hours during the same admission.

Step 5: Failed Reduction → Emergency Surgery — If reduction fails, the hernia remains irreducible, or any concern for strangulation: proceed to emergency surgery. Do NOT repeat the reduction attempt. The window for safe reduction is past.

Step 6: Open Exploration — Incision over the hernia (groin for inguinal/femoral, midline for ventral). Open the sac carefully. Note the colour of the peritoneal fluid (clear = viable, serosanguinous = at risk, dark or bloody = gangrenous). Release the constriction at the hernia neck.

Step 7: Assess Bowel Viability — Deliver the obstructed loop. Assess: colour (pink vs dusky vs black), peristalsis (present vs absent), mesenteric pulsations. If viability is uncertain: wrap in warm saline-soaked packs and wait 5-10 minutes. Repeat assessment.

Step 8: Bowel Resection (if needed) — If non-viable: resect the affected segment. For limited segment: resect through the groin incision. For extensive resection: extend the incision or perform a separate midline laparotomy. Primary anastomosis if the patient is stable and contamination is minimal.

Step 9: Hernia Repair — Viable bowel, no contamination: mesh repair (Lichtenstein for inguinal, McVay for femoral, onlay/sublay for ventral). Non-viable bowel or contamination: primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice for inguinal, McVay for femoral). NO mesh in contaminated fields.

Step 10: Postoperative Care — Continue IV fluids until bowel function returns (passing flatus/stool). NG tube on free drainage. Monitor for progression to strangulation (worsening pain, fever, lactic acidosis). VTE prophylaxis. Wound care. Discuss delayed elective mesh repair if tissue repair was performed.

Step 11: Reduction en Masse Warning — If the patient reports a history of forceful manual reduction followed by persistent obstruction, suspect reduction en masse. Abdominal CT will show the hernia sac in the preperitoneal space with persistent obstruction. The patient requires emergency exploration to reduce the sac and assess viability. The incarcerated bowel may now be strangulated because it was pushed through the tight ring and is still compressed by it, even though the sac is no longer palpable externally.

Step 12: Decision for Stoma Creation — If an obstructed hernia requires bowel resection, the decision to create a stoma depends on: (a) degree of contamination (gross purulent/feculent contamination → stoma), (b) patient hemodynamic stability (unstable → damage control + stoma), (c) bowel wall edema (severe edema precluding safe anastomosis → stoma), (d) anticipated length of resection (extensive resection → stoma), and (e) nutritional status (malnourished → stoma). A diverting loop ileostomy is the preferred stoma type for small bowel resection; end colostomy for left colon resection.

managementPearls.pearl: The single most important clinical distinction in hernia emergencies is between an obstructed hernia (colicky pain, viable bowel, can wait a few hours for resuscitation) and a strangulated hernia (constant severe pain, ischemic bowel, must go to theatre immediately). Mistaking strangulation for obstruction is a catastrophic error.
managementPearls.evidence: WSES Guidelines 2022; East African Hernia Registry data

managementPearls.pearl: Gentle manual reduction of an obstructed inguinal hernia under sedation has a success rate of 50-60% in early presentations (<6 hours). Use the 'taxonomy' technique: patient in Trendelenburg, steady sustained pressure with the palm of the hand over the hernia mass (not fingertip pressure). Reduction is felt as a sudden 'give' as the contents slip back through the external ring.
managementPearls.evidence: East African Hernia Registry 2018; Maltby B, Hernia 2016

managementPearls.pearl: After successful manual reduction of an obstructed hernia, admit the patient and schedule elective repair within 24-48 hours. Do NOT discharge the patient and schedule for later — the risk of re-obstruction or progression to strangulation before the elective date is 10-15% within 48 hours.
managementPearls.evidence: Obesity and hernia recurrence studies; East African Hernia Registry

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan setting, many patients with obstructed hernia present after 12-24 hours of symptoms. In these patients, the likelihood of successful manual reduction is low (20-30%), and the risk of gangrenous bowel is 20-30%. Proceed directly to surgical exploration rather than attempting reduction.
managementPearls.evidence: Local surgical experience; African multicenter study 2019

managementPearls.pearl: When assessing bowel viability during surgery for obstructed hernia, remember that the segment of bowel at the level of the constriction ring (the 'neck' of the sac) is the most ischemic part. The bowel on either side of the ring may appear viable while the constricted segment is non-viable. Resect at least 2-3 cm beyond the visible transition zone.

If an obstructed hernia is found to contain omentum rather than bowel, the omentum may be edematous and friable. Resect the incarcerated omental segment between ligatures — leaving ischemic omentum in the sac can lead to fat necrosis, abscess, or persistent pain. The omentum can be resected through the groin incision.

For obstructed femoral hernia, the 'low approach' (infra-inguinal incision over the mass) allows direct access to the hernia sac. If the contents are viable, reduce them and repair the defect. If non-viable, the low approach is insufficient — extend the incision or convert to a laparotomy to safely mobilize and resect the bowel.

In obstructed umbilical or incisional hernias, the laparotomy incision should be placed to allow access to both the hernia sac and the peritoneal cavity for bowel mobilization. A midline incision that incorporates the old scar is usually best. Never attempt to resect a long segment of ischemic bowel through a small hernia incision.

The 'reduction en masse' is a surgical catastrophe that occurs when forceful manual reduction pushes the entire hernia sac with incarcerated bowel back into the abdomen, past the constriction ring. The obstruction persists but the hernia is no longer palpable. Suspect this when a patient has persistent obstruction after an apparently 'successful' reduction. CT shows the sac in the preperitoneal space. Emergency laparotomy is required.
managementPearls.evidence: Moynihan B, Abdominal Operations 1905; Case series 2020

managementPearls.pearl: In obstructed hernia with short segment of non-viable omentum only (no bowel), the omentum can be resected through the groin incision. Ligate the omental vessels carefully and divide between clamps. Do NOT leave ischemic omentum in the abdomen — it can cause fat necrosis, abscess, or be a nidus for adhesions. After omental resection, the hernia defect can be repaired with mesh as the field is clean.
managementPearls.evidence: Bittner R et al., Hernia 2014; IEHS Guidelines

managementPearls.pearl: After successful manual reduction of an obstructed hernia, the patient should pass large amounts of flatus and stool within 30-60 minutes. If the patient does NOT pass flatus despite a successful reduction, obtain an abdominal X-ray to confirm that the obstruction has truly been relieved. A persistent air-fluid level pattern suggests reduction en masse or another obstructing lesion.

In obstructed hernia with a prolonged history (>24 hours), the bowel segment may have undergone ischaemic preconditioning — brief periods of partial ischaemia followed by reperfusion that activate protective cellular pathways (HIF-1α, VEGF, heme oxygenase-1). These segments may appear worse than they actually are and may recover with 10-15 minutes of warm saline packing. Give the bowel the benefit of the doubt in borderline cases, but mark the area with a silk suture and plan a second-look laparotomy if any doubt persists.
managementPearls.evidence: Murry CE et al., Circulation 1986; bowel ischaemia experimental studies

managementPearls.pearl: The 'obstruction triad' in elderly patients with known hernia: an irreducible hernia + vomiting + absolute constipation should be assumed to be an obstructed femoral hernia until proven otherwise, especially in women. Femoral hernias in the elderly are easily missed because the mass is small and hidden in the groin crease. A lack of a palpable lump does NOT exclude a femoral hernia — CT is mandatory if clinical suspicion is high.
managementPearls.context: Obstructed femoral hernia in elderly women
managementPearls.evidence: Femoral hernia case series; HerniaSurge Guidelines 2018

operativeNuances.nuance: During reduction of an obstructed inguinal hernia, if the bowel is viable but edematous and difficult to reduce, incise the external ring (or the internal ring for indirect hernia) with a hernia knife or scissors to widen the constriction. This releases the strangulation and allows the bowel to be reduced without trauma. Be careful of the inferior epigastric vessels when incising the internal ring medially.
operativeNuances.evidence: Shouldice technique; Malek S et al., Hernia 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: After reducing the obstructed bowel, assess the colour of the serosal surface carefully. A 'double ring' sign — two concentric rings of congestion or ischemia at the points of constriction (afferent and efferent limbs) — indicates the segment was under significant tension. These areas should be closely observed or oversewn with interrupted seromuscular sutures to prevent delayed perforation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Trauma surgery principles; bowel ischemia assessment guidelines

operativeNuances.nuance: If the obstructed bowel is deemed viable but has significant oedema, consider returning it to the abdomen and placing a drain in the hernia sac. The oedema may take 24-48 hours to resolve, and fluid accumulation (seroma/hematoma) in the sac can mimic a persistent mass. The drain confirms resolution of oedema and prevents seroma formation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Local surgical practice; Kockerling F et al., Hernia 2018

operativeNuances.nuance: In the open repair after obstructed hernia, if the surgeon elects to use mesh (viable bowel, no contamination), the mesh must be placed in a well-vascularized plane. The preperitoneal or retromuscular plane is preferred over onlay placement because the tissue planes are less traumatized and better vascularized, reducing infection risk.

When delivering the obstructed bowel through a groin incision, if the bowel is distended and difficult to reduce, the surgeon can aspirate the gas through a purse-string suture using a 16-gauge needle attached to suction. This decompresses the bowel, making reduction easier and reducing the risk of bowel injury during manipulation. After decompression, close the enterotomy with the purse-string suture.
operativeNuances.evidence: Bowel decompression technique; Malek S et al., Hernia 2004

operativeNuances.nuance: In the preperitoneal approach for obstructed inguinal hernia, enter the preperitoneal space through a lower midline or Pfannenstiel incision. This approach allows: (1) visualization of the hernia from inside the abdomen, (2) easy delivery of the incarcerated bowel, (3) assessment of viability, (4) bowel resection if needed, and (5) placement of a large preperitoneal mesh covering both groins. This is the preferred approach for obstructed hernia when laparoscopic expertise is unavailable.
operativeNuances.evidence: Stoppa RE, World J Surg 1989; Schumpelick V, Hernia repair 2012

operativeNuances.nuance: If the obstructed bowel loop is viable but the constriction ring ('groove') at the neck of the sac is deep and appears ischemic, oversew the affected serosal area with interrupted 4-0 silk sutures (imbricating the serosa over the ischemic spot). This prevents delayed perforation of the compromised area, which can occur 2-4 days postoperatively even though the bowel appeared otherwise viable at the initial operation.
operativeNuances.evidence: Serosal patching technique; Bowel viability principles

operativeNuances.nuance: For obstructed femoral hernia where the constriction is extremely tight, the best approach to release the incarcerated contents is the 'low approach' with lacunar ligament division. Make a 5 cm vertical incision directly over the femoral mass. Dissect down to the hernia sac. Identify the lacunar ligament medially. Palpate for the corona mortis (aberrant obturator artery) — if a pulse is felt, ligate the vessel before dividing the ligament. Divide the lacunar ligament in a medial-to-lateral direction using a hernia knife or scalpel under direct vision. The constriction will release immediately, and the contents can be reduced.
operativeNuances.evidence: McVay CB, Ann Surg 1966; Lytle WJ, Ann R Coll Surg Engl 1957

operativeNuances.nuance: In the preperitoneal (Chevrel) approach for obstructed inguinal hernia, a 6 cm transverse incision is made 2 cm above the inguinal ligament. The external oblique is not opened. Instead, the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles are split in the line of their fibers, and the preperitoneal space is entered. The hernia is approached from above the neck of the sac. This approach allows reduction of the incarcerated bowel without entering the inguinal canal and is particularly useful when the anterior approach is scarred from previous surgery.
operativeNuances.evidence: Chevrel JP, Hernia repair techniques 1998; Nyhus LM, Surg Clin North Am 1993

prognosis.naturalHistory: An obstructed hernia will not resolve spontaneously — the bowel cannot pass through the narrowed hernia neck. Without intervention, the natural progression is: continued colicky pain for 6-12 hours, then the pain becomes constant as venous congestion develops (transition to strangulation), then ischemic pain and gangrene develop over the next 6-12 hours. The total window from obstruction to gangrene is approximately 12-24 hours.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After successful manual reduction followed by elective mesh repair: 2-4% recurrence at 5 years. After emergency surgery with mesh (viable bowel): 3-5% recurrence at 2 years. After emergency surgery with primary tissue repair (no mesh, contaminated): 10-15% recurrence at 2 years. Delayed elective mesh repair after initial tissue repair: recurrence rate of the initial tissue repair (10-15%) plus the second procedure.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Obstructed hernia itself does not increase cancer risk. However, in patients >50 years, a hernia that becomes obstructed may contain an underlying malignancy that has 'dropped' into the sac (e.g., a colonic tumor). Colonoscopy is recommended 6-8 weeks after recovery if the hernia contained colon. In female patients, consider pelvic ultrasound to exclude ovarian pathology.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: An episode of obstructed hernia is a painful, frightening experience. Patients who undergo successful manual reduction followed by elective repair have an excellent outcome with minimal impact on long-term quality of life. Patients who require emergency surgery with bowel resection have a more prolonged recovery and may have persistent gastrointestinal symptoms (altered bowel habits, diarrhea). Stoma formation significantly impairs quality of life.
prognosis.mortality: Obstructed hernia managed with successful reduction + elective repair: mortality <0.5%. Obstructed hernia requiring emergency surgery without bowel resection: 2-5% mortality. Obstructed hernia requiring bowel resection: 8-12% mortality. Progression to strangulation before surgery: 15-25% mortality. Mortality is primarily from sepsis and multi-organ failure in patients with delayed presentation.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: duration >12 hours before presentation, femoral hernia (strangulates faster than inguinal), failed manual reduction, need for bowel resection, age >70 years, ASA III-IV, diabetes, and significant contamination at surgery. Protective factors: early presentation (<6 hours), successful manual reduction, inguinal (vs femoral) hernia, viable bowel at surgery, and elective mesh repair.
prognosis.pathophysiologicalTimeline: The obstructed hernia follows a predictable timeline: 0-6 hours — colicky pain, bowel viable, high chance of successful reduction (50-60%); 6-12 hours — pain becomes more constant, venous congestion begins, reduction success drops to 30-40%; 12-24 hours — venous congestion established, bowel wall edematous, reduction rarely successful (10-20%), bowel resection likely; >24 hours — high likelihood of non-viable bowel, emergency laparotomy with resection almost certain. Each hour of delay beyond 12 hours increases the risk of bowel resection by approximately 5%.
prognosis.intestinalAdaptation: After successful relief of obstruction (with or without resection), the remaining bowel undergoes adaptive changes: crypt cell hyperplasia increases absorptive surface area, epithelial cell turnover accelerates, and the bowel lumen dilates to accommodate increased flow. This adaptation takes 4-8 weeks and may be associated with transient diarrhea, cramping, and food intolerance during this period.

presentingComplaints: A known hernia that has suddenly become irreducible
Cramping abdominal pain that comes in waves
Nausea with vomiting that may become feculent
Absolute constipation — no stool or flatus for more than 24 hours

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Progression to strangulation/peritonitis -> SEVERE; simple obstruction with viable contents -> URGENT (but non-severe)

history_questions.question: Did you have a hernia before that used to go back in but now it is stuck?
When did the hernia become irreducible?
Do you have cramping abdominal pain that comes in waves?
When did you last pass stool or flatus?
Have you vomited? What colour is the vomit (green, brown, clear)?
Is your abdomen becoming distended (swollen)?
Do you have a fever or chills?
Is the pain getting progressively worse?
Do you have any pain at the hernia site itself?
How long have you had the hernia — weeks, months, years?
Have you had any previous hernia surgery?
Have you ever had an episode like this before that resolved on its own?
What type of hernia was it — groin, belly button, or at a scar?
Have you tried to push the hernia back in yourself? Did you use any special method?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes, hypertension, or heart disease?
Are you on any blood thinners (aspirin, warfarin, clopidogrel)?
Have you been able to drink any fluids?
Is the skin over the hernia changing colour (red, dusky)?
Do you feel dizzy or light-headed?
Have you had any weight loss recently?
Have you taken any traditional medicine or herbal remedies for the hernia?
Did the pain start as cramping (waves) and then become constant?
Have you had any previous similar episodes that resolved on their own?
Do you have any known umbilical, incisional, or femoral hernia as well?
Have you passed any blood in your stool or noticed dark/tarry stools?
Do you have any abdominal scars from previous surgeries?
Have you had any previous episodes of bowel obstruction from any cause?
Do you take any medications for constipation or have you been using enemas?
Have you had any unintentional weight loss or loss of appetite?
Do you have any fever, chills, or night sweats?
Have you been able to drink or keep fluids down?
How much urine have you passed in the last 12-24 hours?
Have you ever been told you have a 'stricture' or narrowing of the bowel from any cause?
Do you have a history of diverticulitis, Crohn's disease, or ulcerative colitis?
Have you had any prior abdominal radiation therapy for cancer?
Do you have any family history of colon cancer or bowel obstruction?
Have you tried any traditional enemas or herbal purgatives at home?
Is there any history of tuberculosis involving the abdomen or lymph nodes?

examination_findings.finding: Irreducible hernia mass — cannot be reduced into the abdomen
Tenderness over the hernia mass on palpation
Cough impulse may be present (if still viable) or weak (if venous congestion)
Signs of bowel obstruction — abdominal distension, tympanic percussion
High-pitched bowel sounds with rushes (early obstruction) or absent (late)
Localized guarding or peritonism over the hernia site
Tachycardia suggesting early systemic response
Fever — low grade in early stages, high grade with strangulation
Overlying skin: may be normal, edematous, or mildly erythematous
Dehydration — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor
Palpable loops of bowel proximal to the hernia (obstructed loops)
Rectal exam — empty rectal ampulla suggesting large bowel obstruction
Hypotension if dehydration severe or strangulation developing
Visible peristaltic waves across the abdominal wall (in thin patients with prolonged obstruction)
Auscultation may reveal a 'bruit' over the hernia if the entrapped bowel has a hyperdynamic mesenteric flow
The hernia mass may feel tense and 'drum-like' due to gas-filled trapped bowel
Rectal exam reveals an empty ballooned rectum in large bowel obstruction from an obstructed femoral hernia
The patient appears restless and distressed from colicky pain — they may be unable to lie still during history taking
The 'obstruction sound': on auscultation, high-pitched 'tinkling' bowel sounds with rushes that coincide with colicky pain episodes — this is characteristic of mechanical small bowel obstruction
Visible peristaltic waves traveling across the abdominal wall in thin patients with prolonged obstruction
The hernia mass may feel 'doughy' if omentum is the incarcerated content, or 'tense and tympanitic' if gas-filled bowel

In femoral hernia obstruction, the mass may be very small (<2 cm) and located deep in the groin crease, easily missed in obese patients — careful, systematic palpation of the femoral triangle is mandatory
The 'gurgle sign': when gentle pressure is applied to the hernia mass, a gurgling sound may be heard as gas and fluid move within the incarcerated bowel loop
Abdominal percussion reveals tympany (gas-filled distended bowel) over the central abdomen and dullness in the flanks if free fluid is present
In thin patients, the obstructed small bowel loops may be visible as 'ladder patterns' across the abdominal wall
Inguinal examination on the unaffected side may reveal a reducible hernia that was previously unknown — always examine both groins

bedside_scores.name: Obstructed Hernia Severity and Progression Score

components.name: Complete obstruction (no flatus or stool for 24h+)

bedside_scores.interpretation: 0-3: simple obstruction, urgent repair within hours; 4-7: high risk of progression, emergency surgery; 8-12: likely strangulation, emergency laparotomy

riskFactors.note: Highest obstruction rate due to tight ring

subtypes.name: Early Obstructed (Viable Contents)
subtypes.management: Urgent surgery within hours (not minutes). IV fluids resuscitation. Nasogastric tube. Attempt gentle manual reduction under sedation (for inguinal). If successful: elective repair in 24-48h. If fails: open exploration. Reduce contents; assess viability. Mesh repair if no contamination.

subtypes.name: Obstructed with Viability At Risk
subtypes.management: Emergency surgery. IV fluids, antibiotics. Open exploration. Assess bowel viability carefully (warm saline packs 5-10min). If non-viable: bowel resection. Primary repair (no mesh) if contaminated. Hernia repair with tissue (Bassini/Shouldice). Consider stoma if extensive resection.

subtypes.name: Obstructed Femoral Hernia
subtypes.management: Emergency surgery. Low groin approach or preperitoneal. Release constriction (incise lacunar ligament — watch for corona mortis). Assess bowel viability. Mesh plug repair if viable; primary McVay repair if contaminated. Very high rate of progression to strangulation — do not attempt manual reduction.

bedside.name: Assessment of hernia reducibility and tenderness
Cough impulse
bedside.indication: present = still viable; absent = concern for strangulation

imaging.name: Groin/Abdominal Ultrasound with Doppler
imaging.indication: assess contents, vascularity, bowel wall thickness, free fluid

imaging.name: Abdominal X-Ray (supine + erect)
imaging.indication: air-fluid levels suggesting obstruction, rule out perforation

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.indication: if diagnosis unclear, to assess obstruction level, bowel viability

interpretation_rules.differentiating_obstructed_vs_strangulat: Obstructed: colicky pain, vomiting, distension, hernia tender but skin normal, cough impulse present/weak. Strangulated: constant severe pain, skin changes, systemic toxicity, absent cough impulse, lactic acidosis.
interpretation_rules.manual_reduction_caution: Only attempt gentle manual reduction in early obstruction (<6h) and with sedation. Never force reduction — can reduce non-viable bowel (reduction en masse) or perforate the bowel.
interpretation_rules.surgical_timing: An obstructed hernia is a surgical emergency within hours — not minutes. Use the time to resuscitate with IV fluids, correct electrolytes, and start antibiotics. Do not delay beyond 2-4 hours if reduction fails.
interpretation_rules.mesh_vs_tissue_repair: If the bowel is viable and no contamination: mesh repair acceptable. If viability uncertain or any contamination: primary tissue repair only. Never place mesh in a potentially contaminated field.

management.protocolReference: World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) Guidelines for Obstructed Hernia (2022)
management.initial: IV fluids resuscitation. NG tube decompression. NBM. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Catheterize for urine output monitoring. Analgesia. Attempt gentle manual reduction (if inguinal, early, <6h, with sedation). If successful: schedule elective repair within 24-48h. If fails: emergency surgery.
management.definitive: Open exploration of the hernia. Approach via the groin (inguinal/femoral) or directly over the hernia. Open the sac, reduce the contents. Assess bowel viability. If viable and no contamination: mesh repair (Lichtenstein or plug for inguinal; McVay for femoral). If viability uncertain: warm saline packs for 10 minutes. If non-viable: bowel resection (need for laparotomy to mobilize bowel). Primary tissue repair (Bassini/Shouldice). No mesh. Consider stoma if contamination significant.
management.postop: NG tube until bowel function returns. Continue IV fluids and antibiotics. Monitor for signs of progression to strangulation. Wound care. DVT prophylaxis. Stoma care if created.

pathways.criteria: obstruction <12h, no systemic toxicity, no peritonitis
pathways.treatment: Attempt gentle manual reduction with sedation. If successful: elective repair in 24-48h. If fails: urgent open exploration, reduce contents, assess viability. Mesh repair if clean.

pathways.criteria: obstruction >12h, localized tenderness, mild systemic signs
pathways.treatment: Emergency surgery. Open exploration. Warm saline packs. Bowel resection if non-viable. Primary tissue repair (no mesh). Consider stoma.

pathways.criteria: severe pain, skin changes, peritonitis, systemic toxicity
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Bowel resection. Primary tissue repair. No mesh. Stoma if contamination. ICU care.

operative_templates.procedure: Open Exploration of Obstructed Inguinal Hernia
operative_templates.indication: Obstructed inguinal hernia: failed manual reduction or high risk of strangulation
operative_templates.approach: Open — inguinal incision with extension to gridiron if needed
operative_templates.incision: Oblique inguinal incision over the hernia, extended medially along the inguinal canal
steps: Oblique groin incision over the inguinal canal
Open external oblique aponeurosis, identify the cord and hernia sac
Open the hernia sac carefully — note the colour of peritoneal fluid
Release any constriction at the external ring (incise external oblique if needed)
Deliver the obstructed bowel loop from the sac
Assess bowel viability: colour, peristalsis, pulsation of mesenteric vessels
If viable: cover with warm saline pack, reduce into abdomen
If non-viable: tag with stay sutures and extend incision or perform separate laparotomy
Resect non-viable bowel segment and perform anastomosis
Repair the posterior wall — Lichtenstein mesh if no contamination; Bassini/Shouldice if contaminated
Close external oblique, subcutaneous tissue, and skin

dischargeCriteria: No fever or signs of sepsis
Educated on activity restrictions and warning signs

patient_education.explanation: An obstructed hernia means that a loop of the bowel has become trapped in the hernia opening and is blocked. The bowel cannot push contents through, leading to cramping pain, vomiting, and abdominal swelling. The blood supply is still OK for now, but it can quickly become cut off (strangulation), which is a life-threatening emergency. That is why we need to operate urgently to relieve the blockage.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital IMMEDIATELY if: the pain becomes constant and severe, the hernia becomes more tender and you cannot push it in, the skin over the hernia turns red or dusky, you develop fever with chills, or you cannot pass stool or gas.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting for 4-6 weeks after surgery. Walking is encouraged. Return to work: desk job 2-3 weeks, manual labour 6-8 weeks. Use scrotal support if swelling in the groin.
patient_education.diet: Start with clear liquids after surgery. Advance to soft foods as tolerated. Eat a high-fiber diet to prevent constipation. Drink 8 glasses of water daily. Use stool softeners if needed.
patient_education.medication: Take all antibiotics as prescribed. Take pain medication as needed. Keep the wound clean and dry. If you had a bowel resection, you will have specific dietary and medication instructions.
patient_education.followUp: Return in 1-2 weeks for wound check. If you had a bowel resection, you may need longer follow-up. If a mesh was placed, be aware of it for future medical care.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure bowel function returned. Wound check. Activity restriction discussed. Educate on distinguishing obstruction from strangulation signs.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check, suture removal. Assess for signs of recurrence or infection.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical review. Ensure no recurrence. Discuss elective repair if manual reduction was done and definitive repair pending.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery assessment. Check for recurrence. Address chronic pain if any. Discharge if well.
after_discharge.3_months: Only if recurrence concerns or chronic pain.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: recurrent irreducible hernia, severe pain, vomiting, fever, wound discharge, or inability to pass stool or gas.
